blurb: The iconic scrub oak of the Front Range foothills — deeply lobed leaves turning gold and russet in fall, acorns that feed everything, and a tough, thicket-forming nature that thrives on dry slopes.
size: ~15–30 ft tall × 15–25 ft wide (often a multi-stem thicket)
seasons: Deeply lobed green leaves turning gold-russet in fall; acorns late summer–fall; bare gray branches in winter
wildlife: Acorns and cover are a wildlife powerhouse — deer, turkeys, jays, squirrels; larval host for many butterflies and moths
deer: Browsed, and acorns eaten — a key wildlife plant
gaps: No clean open-licensed acorn close-up found; acorns are described but the reel shows foliage, habit, and fall color.
